data.title: ZSP:CA - Unhedged S&P 500 ETF Likely To Slide As CAD Appreciates
data.description:
What is ZSP:CA?

The BMO S&P 500 Index ETF (ZSP:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of $20.6 billion CAD that invests in large-cap American listed stocks, with additional minor exposure (under 3%) in global equities outside of the U.S. BMO, and most of the 5 banks, try to provide the same scale of offerings that American fund managers like Vanguard and BlackRock do, and I think they provide cleaner ways to do so in one's portfolio in Canada. BMO's passive offering is very liquid, tracks the S&P 500 very closely, and has a small expense ratio, making it an easy way to get U.S. exposure in Canada. This article seeks to compare this offering with some other larger American fund offerings.

ZSP:CA's benchmark target is the S&P 500 (SPY) the fund has not materially deviated from its benchmark, while also presenting less volatility during selloffs, with a beta of 0.97. VFV:CA or XUS:CA are other similar hedged alternatives, while ZSP:CA is a not. I rate it a Hold because its unhedged, and I anticipate the Canadian dollar improving given oil strength and geopolitical shifts away from U.S. dominance, so the fund would drag in performance.

Fund Breakdown

ZSP:CA was launched on Nov. 14, 2012 by BMO with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that invested in highly liquid U.S. companies with growth upside. The ETF has successfully tracked the S&P 500 for over 13 years now, showing almost identical returns with a slightly lower Beta.

The fund has an expense ratio of just 0.09%, in line with VFV:CA and slightly above XUS:CA, which is at 0.08%. Additionally, the fund has lots of liquidity, with daily volumes averaging over 146,000 shares traded, which means over $15 million CAD of volume per day. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. Overall, the structure of the fund is solid and investors have been receptive to buying it to get U.S. large cap exposure.

ZSP:CA is different than other Canadian-listed S&P 500 ETFs because it isn't hedged back to CAD, meaning investors get both U.S. equity returns and exposure to FX currency movements. Unlike hedged alternatives like XSP:CA, ZSP:CA does not use derivatives to hedge exchange rate risk, so returns improve when the U.S. dollar strengthens. Its why the fund has been an under-performer over the past year, and I think it may continue to drag XSP:CA, the hedged version.

With the Canadian economy backed by oil, and with a stable government, I expect CAD to slowly drift towards 1.30, especially with risk that the petro dollar may yield to the yuan. Expanding on that point, my bullish CAD thesis is a structural shift building on the oil trade. WCS should continue to strengthen as Trans-Mountain expands capacity in early 2027 which would reduce the discount and boost income, with higher prices driving higher GDP through producer cash flows. As infrastructure improves and the discount gets smaller, foreign capital should enter into new Canadian builds, like for LNG Canada Phase 2, boosting long-term export capacity. Additionally, diversifying away from the USD should weaken USD demand, particularly if the FED starts moving toward a FED Funds rate of 3%. The Bank of Canada is supported by commodity strength and has had a stable rate around 2.25%-2.50% which is supporting my take on a favorable backdrop for CAD. Therefore, all else equal, I think the unhedged ZSP:CA would continue to underperform a bit versus the hedged options.

Previous Results and Fund Portfolio

When comparing ZSP:CA's portfolio, there are limited differences in the portfolio breakdown to SPY. As of Mar. 18, 2026, the portfolio greatly resembles SPY, with top holdings like Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and sector diversification in line with the benchmark. Ultimately, this fund is almost the same as SPY, which gives investors confidence that the fund's past performance will continue to align with the S&P 500's stocks in the future.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.97 means that the fund will have slightly less volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. The fund has almost had identical returns, but has seen slight outperformance this year, while lagging the index a little bit over the last year. The ETF has a Sharpe ratio of 1.51, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, though over 1 is good as an investment. Another key risk in investing in this fund is that the S&P 500 slides, which would make the investment depreciate. Recently, the market has seen shaky returns over the past six months, with volatile US trade policy and more uncertainty surrounding the war, and I can't predict what will happen next.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is that this fund is an easy way to get U.S. large cap exposure in Canada. The fund has a low expense ratio that is comparable or better than peers, the fund is very liquid to the tune of $15 million CAD traded per day, and the beta is slightly below the benchmark it tracks. However, I think it will continue to underperform hedged versions as Canadian currency strengthens and therefore, I rate this fund a Hold.

data.title: Super Micro: From Undervalued To Uninvestable
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Just last month, I called Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) the most misunderstood AI stock in the market and upgraded it to a Strong Buy.

Today, the stock is down over 27%.

Clearly, I was wrong to be bullish.

But what has changed is not the fundamental AI story or even the company’s positioning in the AI infrastructure stack.

What’s changed is trust.

New allegations have resurfaced that a SMCI director was involved in selling illegal chips, Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to China.

The market no longer trusts SMCI, and with good reason. There were plenty of signs, and I chose to ignore them.

It’s a hard lesson to learn, but a lesson learned.

SMCI has become uninvestable in my book.

From Underrated To Uninvestable

In my previous piece, I laid out what I considered to be a logical thesis.

The market was pricing SMCI as a low-margin commodity hardware vendor, while the company was clearly transitioning into a full-stack AI infrastructure provider.

My original thesis was supported by three key pillars:

  • Explosive AI-driven revenue growth

  • Early signs of margin expansion via DCBBS solutions

  • A structural shift toward rack-scale AI factory deployments

None of that has really changed; the problem is that SMCI already had a track record of issues, and that just got much worse.

First, it was the audit of its financials; now, it’s alleged criminal wrongdoing by its co-founder.

The Allegations: Why This Matters

According to a recent report, company insiders, specifically a co-founder and an employee, have been implicated in the smuggling of restricted Nvidia chips. However, it's important to note Super Micro has not been named as a defendant in this indictment.

U.S. officials allege the trio went to great lengths to hide their actions from both U.S.-based server manufacturers and export control authorities, even using hair dryers to remove labels and serial numbers ​from the real machines and placing them on dummy machines left behind after the real machines had been shipped to China.

Source: SA Press Release

This is not a minor issue, especially given the company’s track record.

In today’s geopolitical environment, where AI chips are effectively strategic assets, these are serious allegations.

What’s Next For SMCI?

This is the real question, and the answer is not good.

The stock is facing real governance concerns, and this doesn't seem like something that will get quickly cleared up and cause a sudden reversal.

Even if this is as bad as it gets in terms of negative press, investor confidence has been decimated, and you can no longer value SMCI like a regular company.

More importantly, all eyes are now on customers. This is the real risk.

Any signs of hyperscaler hesitation, delayed orders, or diversification away from SMCI would validate what the market is already beginning to price in.

Longer term, the company must do two things: restore trust and prove margins can scale.

But this seems very difficult right now. It would take a complete upheaval of the company’s management and very, very strong earrings.

But again, the risk is not just the loss of trust by investors; it's the loss of trust by clients.

What Should Investors Do?

I’ve been known to be somewhat of a contrarian, and perhaps this is why I’ve tracked SMCI so closely. However, this is one instance where you don’t want to fight the trend.

I’m happy to take big risks if there’s a big potential payout, but that is no longer the case for SMCI.

The best-case scenario is maybe that somehow the people involved in the chip-smuggling case are cleared.

But even if that happens, you can’t recover the trust of investors.

The delayed financials were strike one. In hindsight, that should have been enough. This is strike two, but I’m not waiting for a third one.

Final Thoughts

I am downgrading SMCI to a Sell, of course, even though the damage has already been done. TA or fundamental analysis won’t do much to help here.

I consider SMCI uninvestable at this point.

With that said, we may very well see days of double-digit gains and short squeezes.

But it’s not something we can reasonably anticipate or invest in.

With thousands of quality, honest companies out there, I see no reason to buy SMCI at this point.

data.title: Target Hospitality Still Aims At Its Potential Core Recovery And Growth Drivers
data.description:
Only two months after the publication of my previous coverage, Target Hospitality Corp. (TH) continued to show its resilience despite the unfavorable things it has encountered in the past year. Its value increased by almost 10%, which justifies my Buy rating, small but continuous. Now, macro headwinds are emerging and appear riskier, but I believe that TH may take advantage of them. Also, fundamentals are still robust with its debt-free Balance Sheet. Valuation and technicals are still in sync and show some upside potential.

TH Q4 2025: Recovery Sustained

In the past year or two, Target Hospitality Corp. has been at its low point due to the contract extinguishment of Pecos Children Center or PCC. But it the second half, TH proved it could rise from every challenge after opening itself to other niches. This paid off as it continued to regain its footing, as shown in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $89.8M, up by 7.3% YoY from $83.7M. This YoY growth was higher than in my previous coverage at only 4.4%, which shows sustained improvement. Services income and specialty retail income weakened. This can tell us that it may still need some time to fully recover from the impact of contract extinguishments. If you look at its revenue per segment, its revenues from government and hospitality services segments fell. However, its construction fees, mainly from workforce hospitality solutions or WHS, brought additional revenues of $32.5M. This also shows that its recovery efforts and strategies have become fruitful as it expanded to other niches.

However, its operating costs and expenses rose. Its cost of services doubled, showing that it was not entirely shielded from inflationary headwinds. As a result, it incurred an operating loss. On a lighter note, one must remember that a huge portion of its costs and expenses were associated with construction service activities as well as the extinguishment of its PCC contract, which used to drive its margins. But for 2026, TH anticipates better margins as its Workforce Hub Contract transitions to a service-focused revenue and higher-margin model. This may be supported by WHS contracts, which are expected to scale and the completion of the Dilley Contract ramp-up phases.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation in the country must still be considered since it affects the budget of companies or groups taking the services and solutions of TH. New tariffs may exacerbate it due to their tendency to raise domestic prices, particularly imports. This may increase the cost pressures of TH since inflation and tariffs may raise the prices of construction materials. The ingredients of the food it serves as part of its housing package may become more expensive. With that, its pricing flexibility and margins may be squeezed.

Middle East Tension: A Double-Edged Sword

The escalation of the tension in Iran sent oil prices back to their 13-month highs. Currently, the prices of Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) per barrel are $108.48 and $95.47, respectively. In response, the prices of refined products like fuel may become more expensive. These may have various impacts on TH.

On the negative side, oil price spikes can hurt the economy due to the multiplier effect. Higher oil prices may lead to higher inflation due to higher fuel prices. This may immediately impact shipping, manufacturing, industrial, and logistics. These are large sectors, which may easily affect economic activity. With that, TH may face higher cost pressures on transportation, construction, and day-to-day activities.

On the positive side, higher oil prices may increase upstream activities like oil exploration and production. This usually leads to increased drilling, especially in oil-abundant locations like Texas. So, there may be an influx of demand for worker housing and remote accommodations. TH may take advantage of the situation since it has already been signing contracts with workforces related to energy infrastructure and power plants. With the current range of oil prices, I believe that the sharper side of the sword is more on the positive side. As long as it stays below $120, things may stay good for TH.

Mitigants/Drivers

I understand that the skyrocketing oil prices may have a mixed impact on TH. We cannot yet guarantee that it will purely benefit TH. But at least, TH boasts strengths to protect its business and potentially drive growth.

One aspect to consider is the fact that TH is no longer a pure oil play anymore. For a long time, it focused highly on oil workforce. But in recent years, it has also accommodated other sectors like the government. But what is more interesting is its increased engagement with power infrastructure and data centers. These two are interconnected and may drive growth in TH. Last month, it announced its second bed expansion to data center community. With additional 400 community beds, TH already accommodates up to 1,050 people. This represents a 320% increase from the initial number of 250 community beds. The pace of its bed expansion highlights the increasing momentum of data center capacity demand.

This also shows the crucial role of TH in the continued digital and AI revolution. Recent statistics show that power demand from data centers alone may triple by 2030. Aside from data centers, power infrastructure may increase as well. Both will benefit TH.

These two aspects may also be a growth catalyst for oil and gas drilling activity. Note that natural gas remains the primary electricity source in the U.S. With that, natural gas drilling activities. This may also require more natural gas pipelines and LNG facilities. This increased energy activities can also lead to more workforce being accommodated by TH in the long run.

More importantly, TH maintains its robust fundamentals to support continued expansion. Its cash and cash equivalents may have dropped dramatically to $8.3M. However, it already has a debt-free Balance Sheet. If you look at its equity, the majority of the amount comes from retained earnings. This shows that its net income or operating cash inflow remains enough to sustain its operating capacity. This also suggests that it has enough capital buffer to support its increased activity.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update my DCF Model. Because revenues exceeded my expectations in my previous coverage, it’s not surprising that my new FCFF increased as well. I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be consistent with my previous analysis. I still got its WACC using the same method. Its market risk premium is still 7.0% to be conservative and price in more risks than just the standard of 5.0%. Lastly, cash and cash equivalents decreased in line with Q4 2025 results. My new TP or target price is $11.72. This is also higher than the current price and shows that there is still some upside potential at its level.

I also updated its P/B ratio. Currently, the stock trades at 2.33x its book value versus the five-year average of 3.14x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $12.25. This is also quite close to my DCF TP, which proves the reasonability and slight cheapness of the stock.

Price BVPS P/B
2021 3.19

1.00

3.19
2022 9.10 2.00 4.53
2023 14.05 3.71 3.79
2024 9.38 4.26 2.20
2025 7.71 3.90 1.98
Average 3.14
TTM 9.10 3.90 2.33

Technicals

TH’s technicals are still quite bullish despite some selling pressures we have seen recently. It also appears resilient to macroeconomic headwinds today, which can be rooted from the potential advantages of oil prices and the defensive impact of its data center community expansion. The stock price trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA also appears to be shifting upwards, which means that recent market sentiments have started to improve.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the uptrend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence. The MACD line is also still above the signal line, which means that buying volume is still increasing. The histogram is within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But the decreasing trend close to zero or neutral warrants some caution because it also suggests that sellers are starting to increase and catch up with buyers. This can be explained by its RSI, which shows that the downtrend was due to the recent overselling that prompted profit-taking. But now that buying room is starting to expand again, the downtrend may become manageable. Investors may also be able to spot an entry point.

Conclusion

Target Hospitality Corp. may still need some time to fully recover, but its data center community expansion and strategic exposure to oil and gas workforces may support it. Robust fundamentals also justify its strength and show enough capital buffer to sustain its increasing capacity. Its valuation is still reasonable and suggests some upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as bullish signals remain evident, with the current momentum normalizing again after the recent overbuying. I’m reiterating my Buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Build A Passive Income Buffer As Recession Odds Rise
data.description:
The best thing to have during a recession is cash.

Cash maintains its value as stock prices drop, and it acts as dry powder when an investor decides to buy stocks at discounted valuations.

The second-best thing to have during a recession is cash flow -- that is, a reliable passive income stream with which to regularly invest in discounted stocks, thereby accelerating the compounding effect.

As the military conflict in the Middle East expands to include energy infrastructure and both sides remain hardened in their resolve, the odds of a global recession rise.

While the US is somewhat insulated due to its status as the largest producer of oil and gas in the world, it's worth acknowledging that the US economy is more fragile than it looks. Growth of jobs and real personal income (excluding transfer payments) are both quite weak and trending in the wrong direction.

Could the spike in oil prices be the straw that breaks the economic camel's back?

All I think a rational investor can conclude is that recession odds are rising, and thus it makes sense to make incremental adjustments to one's capital allocation strategy accordingly.

I'm taking an across-the-board approach to building a passive income buffer, including reliable high-yielders, moderate-yielding compounders, and low-yielding dividend growth ETFs.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • Why the US economy is in dire straits right now with high oil prices, a soft labor market, and falling growth in private sector income.
  • A handful of increasing pessimism that could be contrarian buy signals.
  • A reminder that the primary engine driving this bull market remains firmly in place.
  • Some comments on my current dividend growth buy list.

Onward!

Dire Straits

The state of the US economy is... complicated.

Inflation as measured by the CPI remains too hot, largely due to tariffs and now the oil price spike.

And GDP growth remains pretty decent, largely due to AI infrastructure capex and affluent consumer spending.

And yet, the labor market is as weak as it can get without falling (yet) into sustained job losses:

On a year-over-year basis, job growth has basically halted.

Typically, as you can see above, when nonfarm payrolls growth is above the 20-year average of 0.88%, the US economy is solidly in expansion mode. When it drops below that, the US is usually in recession or on its way toward recession.

Perhaps this time is different, though, because of strengthening productivity growth (more on that below).

Another sign of weakening in the US economy pertains to private sector real income growth. This metric adjusts for inflation and excludes government transfer payments such as Social Security, Medicare, and various welfare programs.

Real Personal Income Ex. Transfers:

Growth in real personal income excluding transfers has been steadily falling since the beginning of 2024 and is now well below its long-term average level.

Perhaps more worryingly, this critical metric of American prosperity has been trending down on a rolling average basis for a long time.

The public sector (government spending) is expanding faster than the private sector, which translates into rising government debt-to-GDP. It's helpful to remember that the private sector is the ultimate source of funds for public sector spending, including transfers.

So, the bottom line: Overall, US economic growth is weakening, making the ongoing expansion more fragile.

Now enter the spike in oil prices as a result of the near-total closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil volumes flow.

This creates about as big of an oil supply shock as you could possibly get.

But given the fact that most of this supply out of the Persian Gulf goes to Asian countries, the US is largely insulated from direct price increases.

The oil market is a global one, which is why the WTI crude oil spot price (primary US oil price) has risen alongside the Brent crude oil spot price (primary international oil price).

You can see that US oil prices are persistently lower than international oil prices by nature of the US being the world's largest producer of oil and gas.

Today, there's a big difference between WTI and Brent crude.

The rest of the world will feel the oil flow disruption more than the US, but we still see an oil price spike in the US as well.

This is worrisome because spikes in the price of oil tend to coincide with or precede recessions.

The basic mechanism, in my understanding, is that higher oil prices cause demand destruction by crowding out other consumer spending, and demand destruction is just another word for broad-based economic contraction. And broad-based economic contraction, if deep and prolonged enough, is another way of saying "recession."

That makes it concerning to see the Middle East conflict expanding and beginning to include energy assets.

The probability of a resolution over the next few months, according to both the options market and prediction markets, appears to be less than 50%.

The market is basically forecasting that a ceasefire won't be achieved until sometime this summer, at the earliest.

At the same time, there's a lot of optimism that oil prices will decline once the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, presumably sometime over the next few months.

The Energy Information Administration forecasts Brent crude returning to the low- to mid-$60 area by the end of this year.

To quote the EIA's Short-Term Energy Outlook:

We forecast the Brent crude oil price will remain above $95/b over the next two months, before falling below $80/b in the third quarter of 2026 and around $70/b by the end of the year. We expect prices to average $64/b in 2027.

If this forecast proves accurate, then I would guess that the US will avoid recession even if other countries around the world slump into recession.

But the longer the war lasts, and the longer the strait is closed, the higher the odds of a US recession this year.

That's exactly what we see in the betting markets:

To be clear, a little over 1 in 3 chance of recession in 2026 still implies the US will likely avoid recession.

Then again, a 1 in 3 chance of recession this year seems like decent enough odds to take seriously.

Contrarian Buy Signals

If we take the "glass two-thirds full" approach, then there are lots of indicators suggesting that this market dip is a great buying opportunity.

Exhibit A: Individual investor sentiment has swung back into net bearish territory.

When the US avoids recession, this metric being in net bearish territory acts as a great contrarian buy signal.

If one had bought the dip in April 2025, for example, they would have been richly rewarded by the rest of the year's S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq Index (QQQ) performance:

Much the same could be said about buying in 2022 or during the market swoons in October 2023 and April 2024.

Here's another contrarian indicator corroborating the AAII survey data: the CNN Fear & Greed Index, aggregating a swathe of metrics to measure investor sentiment, is currently in the "extreme fear" zone.

Anything below a measurement of 25 is considered "extreme fear." The index fell below that level around the time that the US and Israel jointly struck the Iranian regime.

Notice, however, that it had been in decline for a month or so already.

It appears as though the New Year optimism about tariff rollbacks and fiscal stimulus via tax cuts and OBBBA benefits has totally deflated.

Additional tax refunds will largely be soaked up by higher gasoline prices, and broader economic uncertainty is holding the private sector back from a large number of investment projects.

Moreover, higher energy prices will likely result in lower discretionary consumer spending over time.

The question is: Will all of this be enough to push the US into recession, or will it simply be another temporary growth slowdown?

As one last potential contrarian buy signal, take a look at the level of ETF short volume right now:

It's higher than the "Liberation Day" selloff. In fact, it's higher than anytime since COVID-19.

On one hand, short volume is really just a corollary of bearish investor sentiment.

On the other hand, there's a difference in degree here. Short volume is significantly higher today than it was even at the peak of the "Liberation Day" selloff.

If we do get a recession and deeper market selloff, the signs were there.

If we avoid recession, then this will look like an amazing buy signal.

What do you see in the inkblot image?

The Primary Engine of This Bull Market

Let's pause for a moment to remember the primary driver of the current bull market.

It isn't consumer spending, oil profits, or private credit.

It's technology capital expenditures related to the artificial intelligence trend.

Just the big five hyperscalers - Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Oracle (ORCL) - plan to spend more than $660 billion on capex this year, a 60% jump from last year's number.

If the AI infrastructure buildout trend drove the majority of earnings growth last year, then how much more should it drive earnings growth this year?

And while future returns on this gargantuan AI capex investment are unknown, it should be acknowledged that US productivity growth has ramped up over the last few years.

Since 2023, productivity growth has averaged 2.8% and seems to be accelerating. This is the fastest productivity growth since the Internet era of 1996-2004.

Artificial intelligence is coming and will have a positive impact on productivity. These early results are likely enough to ensure continued AI infrastructure spending going forward, even if the rest of the economy weakens.

And as I've remarked in the past, one company's spending is another company's income.

Effectively, the hyperscalers' hefty operating cash flows are being tapped to fuel earnings growth in all kinds of businesses that support the AI infrastructure buildout, from chipmakers to data center developers to air conditioning companies and all sorts of others.

Why, then, is the market no longer reacting positively to the myriad mentions of "AI" on earnings calls?

It's probably due to nothing more than the shift in investor sentiment I talked about in previous weekly articles.

Starting around October 2025, investors began to shift from "only AI" to "anything but AI."

Then came the war in Iran, which has put downward pressure on most stocks.

My Varied Buy List

In my dividend portfolio, I like to balance high-yielding investments with little to no dividend growth with moderate-yielding compounders and low-yielding investments with high expected dividend growth.

Dividend Yield Plausible Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB) 4.1% Mid-Single-Digit
Blackstone (BX) ~4.5% High-Single-Digit to Double-Digit
Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF (CGDG) 2.1% High-Single-Digit
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 3.9% High-Single-Digit
Hercules Capital (HTGC) 13.0% Flat to Low-Single-Digit
Virtus InfraCap US Preferred ETF (PFFA) 9.9% Flat
Rexford Industrial Series C Preferred (REXR.PR.C) 6.8% Flat
FT NASDAQ Technology Dividend ETF (TDIV) ~2.0% High-Single-Digit

Obviously, there are too many buyable securities here to discuss thoroughly, so I'll make my comments brief.

BHB is a regional bank concentrated in Maine, New Hampshire, and Vermont. It's conservatively managed, with a sub-40% payout ratio, a 30-plus year dividend growth record, and disciplined underwriting. This is one of my favorite "hidden gem" dividend compounders. I'd like to buy on the dip.

I discussed BX the last few weeks. It has rebounded a bit after bottoming, but the stock remains undervalued and very buyable, in my view. Private credit headwinds are likely temporary, while we appear to be entering a new upcycle in sentiment about commercial real estate - a specialty for BX.

CGDG's non-US holdings are getting dinged along with most international stock markets, related to the oil price spike. But the ETF owns large, high-quality companies around the world that should weather the storm well.

CTRE is primarily a skilled nursing REIT but has recently begun building a portfolio of senior housing properties as well. The REIT is very low leveraged and well positioned to invest heavily using its strong cost of capital. I expect double-digit bottom line growth for at least the next few years.

HTGC is a BDC that's been punished for its ~35% exposure to software loans. But management is highly shareholder-aligned, underwriting is conservative, and defaults are virtually nil as of year-end. Also, HTGC is trading at one of its cheapest levels since the COVID-19 pandemic:

PFFA is a lightly (~20%) leveraged, actively managed preferred stock fund managed by Jay Hatfield, who's heavily invested in the fund himself. PFFA has significantly outperformed all of its passively managed preferred equity ETF peers while consistently paying a monthly dividend. The ETF did reduce its dividend by a little over 20% during the pandemic, but it has since been making up that lost ground with small increases.

REXR is a Southern California-focused industrial REIT with an investment-grade credit rating. When a preferred stock issued by an investment-grade issuer trades at a 6.8% yield, I take notice. In my view, it's a good idea to have some conservative, moderately high-yielders like REXR's preferred equity in an income portfolio.

Finally, TDIV is an "old tech" ETF of dividend-paying technology and telecom companies that I'm excited to build as a position in my portfolio. The yield is low, but I expect dividend growth to be strong as many of TDIV's holdings benefit from huge AI infrastructure spending.

data.title: Dividend Champion, Contender, And Challenger Highlights: Week Of March 22
data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies that have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend-related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized, and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: Enterprise Products: My Top 10 Reasons To Buy
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve been covering Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) for a while now and since my last analysis, units have appreciated by a strong 16.74%, offering a total return nearing 18% thanks to the current situation in the Middle East that has put the spotlight on the relatively safe US energy industry. Though I do not cover the company as much as other names in the industry, I wanted to share my biggest reasons as to why I believe it can keep being a winner for unitholders.

1. 27 Consecutive Years Of Distribution Growth

Let’s start with the most appealing as EPD has increased its distribution every year for the past 27. With distribution increasing by 3.6%, the yield currently stands at a strong 5.81%, though that is lower than the industry average, it still remains largely over the market’s benchmark. More so, the risk profile of EPD means that distribution is one of the safest around.

2. Record EBITDA And Earnings In 2025

Q4 2025 was a strong finish to a great year as overall revenue stood at $13.79B, a $1.4B beat. EPS came in at $0.75 versus the $0.69 consensus, an 8.7% beat though that is not really the preferred metric to look at for MLPs. FY EBITDA was a record $9.96B, up from $9.90B in 2024. Q4 EBITDA alone was $2.7B, up 4% Y/Y. This isn’t a company coasting. The asset base is generating more cash flow than it ever has, and the growth projects coming online in 2026-2027 should push EBITDA comfortably above $10B.

3. The Backlog

The growth story behind EPD is also interesting, considering its $6B backlog with the majority of these projects being completed in 2026 through late 2027. For example, the Bahia pipeline will become critical in the Permian as the expansion following Exxon's entry into the project will push the capacity from 600K bpd to 1MM by the end of 2027. With other projects, EPD generally has contracted and fee-based assets with predictable cash flows thus management has guided for 2026 growth capex of $2B to $2.5B funded by internal cash flows.

4. The Permian Basin

EPD projects that the Permian Basin will represent 85% of the total 1.3MM barrels per day of US NGL production growth through 2030. EPD is positioned directly in the path of this growth with an integrated system that moves NGLs from the wellhead through fractionation and all the way to the export dock. As Permian producers continue to grow, every incremental barrel of associated gas and NGLs needs the relevant midstream infrastructure, boding well with EPD that has the pipes, the fracs, and the export terminals to capture that growth. It’s a toll-road business in the fastest-growing energy basin in the world.

5. Dominant NGL Export Infrastructure

EPD is al the largest exporter of NGLs from the US, and it’s not close. The Enterprise Hydrocarbons Terminal is being expanded to 1.135MM bpd of dedicated LPG capacity, plus the Neches River Terminal adds ethane and propane flexibility, entrenching EPD's dominance in the space. LPG exports are highly contracted through the end of the decade, meaning the revenue is locked in regardless of short-term commodity swings. Global demand for US LPG continues to grow, driven by petrochemical feedstock needs in Asia and heating demand in Europe. Enterprise’s export position is in my mind an irreplaceable asset.

6. Fee-Based Revenue

One of the most misunderstood aspects of EPD is the revenue model. Around 90% of Enterprise’s gross operating margin comes from fee-based contracts. That means EPD gets paid on volume, not on the price of the commodity flowing through its pipes. When oil drops, EPD's cash flow barely moves because the producers are still shipping. This is fundamentally different from E&P companies, where earnings are directly tied to the commodity price. For income investors, this fee-based stability is what makes the distribution so reliable across cycles.

7. Investment-Grade Credit

EPD ended 2025 with a leverage ratio of 3.3 and consolidated liquidity of $5.2B. The company carries an A- credit rating, which is the highest among midstream MLPs and one of the highest in the entire energy sector. This matters for two reasons, it keeps borrowing costs low, which directly benefits returns on growth projects, and it provides a massive cushion in a downturn. A company with $5.2B in liquidity and 3.3 leverage is not going to be forced into a distribution cut or fire-sale asset sales. In simple words, the balance sheet is a fortress.

8. 10% EBITDA Growth Expected In 2027

Management has guided for modest EBITDA growth in 2026 as capital projects ramp, followed by approximately 10% EBITDA growth in 2027 as the full backlog reaches commercial operation. That’s a meaningful step-up for a company of this scale. If EPD hits $10.9B in EBITDA by 2027, and you apply the current EV/EBITDA multiple, you would get a fair value north of $41 per unit. That’s roughly 10% upside from here before you even count the current distribution yield. I believe that total return of 15%+ annualized through 2027 is on the cards for EPD. That’s not bad for what the market considers a boring income stock.

9. Insider Alignment Through The Partnership Structure

The Duncan family, which founded Enterprise, still owns approximately 32% of the outstanding units. That’s over $20 billion in personal wealth tied directly to the partnership’s performance. When management makes capital allocation decisions, they’re making them with their own money on the line. That kind of alignment is rare in corporate America and explains a lot of Enterprise’s discipline over the decades. They don’t chase bad deals. They don’t over-lever. They don’t cut the distribution. Because it’s also their money.

10. Valuation

EPD is still relatively cheap compared to its peers with its EV/EBITDA of 12.08, closer to Energy Transfer (ET) that is riskier in terms of debt than Kinder Morgan (KMI) and Williams (WMB) at 15.18 and 18.04 respectively. More so, EPD has the best credit profile and the longest distribution growth streak among MLPs, making it the best opportunity for income-seeking investors. And despite the unit price appreciation, PED still has a fair FCF yield at around 4%. EPD is also known for its capability to deploy capital efficiently, as its ROIC is consistently at the higher end of the industry at around 11% and the same could be said for the ROE at around 20%, which is simply fabulous.

Risks

Though the partnership model protects against price volatility, if production were to decline due to lower prices, overall throughput would drop. But right now, I do not see it as possible considering where prices are due to the Iran conflict as well as the fact that even when WTI was below $60, volumes across the system reached record highs, telling us that Permian producers are much more profitable at lower prices than first expected.

Another risk related to the current situation in Iran would be the rise of interest rates, as MLPs generally act as bond proxies in the market. If the yields rise, units of EPD become less attractive relative to risk-free treasuries, initiating selling pressure.

Conclusion

EPD is what I consider the safest midstream company on the market thanks to its incredible credit profile as well as its operational track record. More so the backlog and its position in the Permian make it prone to sustain a higher growth profile than other peers in the industry. With all the positives, I can not go under a Strong Buy rating for Enterprise Products Partners.

data.title: ETJ's Put Options On The S&P 500 Are Finally In-The-Money - Buy At $8.10
data.description:
I would be a large buyer of the Eaton Vance Risk-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETJ), $8.10 real time market price, -1.5%.

There are NO other equity closed-end funds, or CEFs, that write Call options as well as buying Put options on the S&P 500 (SP500) that cover 96% of the fund's value.

That's incredibly defensive, but the protection only comes into play when ETJ's Put options go In-the-Money, which they are now after the S&P 500 has dropped more than -3% just in the past month.

In fact, the S&P 500 has fallen -5.7%, so at an average 15-day expiration, ETJ's Puts should really be kicking in now.

Note: Don't forget, ETJ was the ONLY CEF that was positive at total return market price in 2008.

How ETJ's Option Sleeve Works

The Puts (and written Calls) are placed every few business days and have 1-month expirations. Here is what that looked like (as of 12/31/2025 from ETJ's Annual Report):

So why has ETJ's market price and NAV not been performing as well as it should be in a declining market?

First, ETJ's all US stock portfolio is about 40% in the Mag7 stocks and the Mag 7 stocks have been coming down harder than the S&P 500.

Here are ETJ's Top 10 Holdings and portfolio composition:

The second reason for ETJ's underperformance is that the drops in the S&P 500 have been gradual over the past year, so unless the Put options go In-The-Money within a month (which they are now finally doing), then the Put options don't provide any protection.

In other words, the S&P 500 Put options will start to support ETJ's NAV only after a -3% drop within a month. And that protection, like insurance, covers almost all (96%) of the intrinsic value of ETJ's $611 million all large cap U.S. stock portfolio.

Finally, ETJ will be at probably close to a -10.0% discount after today while offering a +9.5% windfall market yield (paid monthly) over its +8.6% NAV yield. That is extremely attractive in the current market environment.

I don't know who would be selling ETJ at this point, since the more the S&P 500 goes down from here, the better for ETJ's option strategy.

I would be a buyer of ETJ at $8.10/share.

data.title: BUXX: Active ETF With 4.9% Yield, Low Credit Risk And Low Duration
data.description:
This article updates my review of August 2025 in light of current portfolio metrics and recent performance.

BUXX strategy

Strive Enhanced Income Short Maturity ETF (BUXX) is an actively managed fund launched on 08/09/2023 with an expense ratio of 0.26%. BUXX has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.45%, a trailing 12-month yield of 4.91%, a yield-to-worst of 5.06% and pays monthly distributions. As described in the prospectus by Strive Assets Management, the fund invests in U.S. dollar-denominated investment-grade debt securities and seeks to maintain a weighted average maturity of less than two years and a weighted average duration of less than one year.

BUXX primarily invests in residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”), asset-backed securities (“ABS”), collateralized loan, debt and mortgage obligations (“CLOs”, “CDOs”, “CMOs”), corporate debt, U.S. treasuries and government agency securities, and may also invest in funds and BDCs. The fund typically invests more than 25% of its assets in mortgage related instruments, less than 25% in CLOs, and avoid concentration in any other debt sectors. It may also borrow up to one third of its total assets and use derivatives for investing or hedging purposes. Securities are selected and weighted based on market conditions, fundamentals, technicals, interest rates, macroeconomic analysis and liquidity. The turnover rate was 54% in the most recent fiscal year and 83% in the previous year.

Portfolio

The portfolio holds 329 securities. U.S. issuers represent 90.5% of asset value, and investment vehicles structured in the Caiman Islands weigh 7.6%, based on Fidelity data. About 30% of asset value is in securities with an “AAA” S&P rating and 69.7% in securities of lower investment grade.

BUXX has low interest rate risk, with an effective duration of 0.75 years. It means that for every 1% change in interest rates, the price is expected to change by approximately 0.75% in the opposite direction.

The portfolio is focused on securitized debt instruments (64.2%) and also has significant weight in corporate bonds (25.4%). Treasuries represent less than 3% of assets.

The next table lists the top 10 holdings as an example from 3/20/2026. They may have changed by the time you read this.

Performance

BUXX has a total return of 15.4% since its inception about 2.5 years ago, outperforming by 2.7 percentage points the short-term treasury benchmark iShares 0–1 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHV).

The gap is only 41 bps over the past 12 months:

SBUXX has gained 1.3% in price since inception, while SHV is close to flat:

The 6-month average distribution has been close to flat in two years, while short-term treasury yields are more than 30% down, as plotted on the next two charts.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of BUXX and five short duration investment grade bond ETFs:

  • JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST)
  • PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (MINT)
  • SPDR Portfolio Short Term Corporate Bond ETF (SPSB)
  • nvesco Ultra Short Duration ETF (GSY)
  • Janus Henderson Short Duration Income ETF (VNLA)

This list is not intended to be exhaustive.

BUXX has the second highest expense ratio, and is the smallest and least liquid of these funds. It has the second highest yield (behind VLNA) and is in the middle of the pack for total return and volatility.

Takeaway

Strive Enhanced Income Short Maturity ETF has a 5% yield from short term debt investment grade securities. BUXX has a focus on securitized debt, in particular mortgage related instruments and collateralized obligations. BUXX is in the average compared to its peers based on yield, return and risk. Collateralized loan obligations ETFs are a compelling alternative in investment-grade debt. In particular, Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) has a better total return in this time frame (17.26%), almost the same yield-to-worst (5%) and an even lower effective duration (0.1 year). Nonetheless, some investors may prefer BUXX or its peers listed above for their broader diversification in debt sectors.

data.title: BBCA Versus SPY: For Canada, Things Will Get Worse Before They Get Better
data.description:
I like Canada. I believe they’re one of our greatest allies. Growing up, I could see Canada from my window. For my 19th birthday, Canada was my place to go (so I could legally enjoy a few drinks.) Essentially, Canada is a country I have a soft spot for and I do not agree with the Trump Administration putting tariffs on them. Yes, there is a massive trade imbalance, but that trade imbalance actually favors the United States (more on that in a moment).

Unfortunately, conditions for Canada are likely to get worse before they get better, and as a result, I must rate JPMorgan BetaBuilders Canada ETF (BBCA) a sell for now. This is one of those cases where I wish I could justify an investment, but I simply can’t. Should tensions with Canada ease and if a trade deal looks reachable, I would likely upgrade my rating to a hold at the very least. For now, however, it seems that the global trade war will continue.

The United States, on the other hand, remains strong overall. While I am seeing some things that worry me regarding the American economy, I don’t feel a need to exit the market. As such, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) remains a buy in my book. That said, I do advise investors to monitor economic indicators and prepare defensive strategies.

The Illusionary Trade Deficit

Few countries have been hit as hard by the Trump Administration as Canada. Various parties within the Administration have alleged that Canada has been ripping the United States off. Yet, as I’ve explored elsewhere, America’s trade deficit with Canada is all too often misrepresented. We can’t compare Canada head-to-head with the United States because America’s population is about 9 times larger than Canada's. Expecting a country of ~42 million to consume as much or more than a country home to ~340 million is patently unfair and perhaps a bit ludicrous.

Instead, we should break down the amount of American goods Canadians buy on a per capita basis. Canada imported roughly $314.5 billion in goods from the United States in 2025. Broken down per capita, this amounts to nearly $9,000 worth of American goods purchased per Canadian. Meanwhile, the United States purchased $383 billion worth of Canadian goods, which breaks down to about $1,120 per American. The U.S. enjoys a services surplus and sent ~$90 billion worth of services to Canada in 2024, up 4.5% ($3.9 billion) from 2023. U.S. services imports from Canada in 2024 were a mere $57.0 billion, up 3.6% ($2.0 billion) from 2023.

Add it all up and Canada is spending far more on American goods and services on a per capita basis. If anyone has a right to complain about the trade deficits, Canada has the stronger argument, in my opinion. Ultimately, the United States enjoys the stronger position in the trade relationship, bolstering American markets, including SPY.

Canada’s Economy Continues To Deteriorate

Unfortunately, Canada is on recession watch because the economy has contracted in two of the last three quarters, including the last quarter in 2025. A technical recession occurs when an economy contracts for two quarters in a row. Should the economy contract in the current quarter, Canada would end up in a technical recession.

As illustrated above, Canada’s unemployment rate is quite high, 6.7% in 2025. While unemployment has cooled a bit in recent weeks, it’s still elevated from a historical perspective. Further, Canada has shed over 100,000 full-time jobs so far in 2026. Consumer spending may be weakening as well, with Canadian consumers increasingly turning to private labels and emphasizing value. (We are seeing similar trends in the U.S., for the record.)

The United States economy, meanwhile, remains strong, at least as far as the headline numbers go. The unemployment rate did tick up to 4.4% in February, from 4.3% in January, but this is still well below Canada’s unemployment rate. American exports to Canada weigh in at 15.14% share of all American exports, the first time that this number has dropped below 16% in quite some time. This has been fueled by Canadian consumer boycotts. Further, Canadian travelers to the United States plummeted by more than 20% to 4.2 million. American visitors to Canada also declined, although only by 7.5%, good for 1.6 million visits. Given how massive the American population is compared to Canada, it’s rather surprising that the gap between the two is so high.

Unfortunately, I believe Canada’s economy will continue to struggle unless it can ink an equitable trade deal with the United States. Canada has been trying to diversify away from dependence on the United States, but this is far easier said than done. America is facing headwinds as well, but despite the turmoil, the United States still remains one of the safer havens to park wealth in.

I do worry about the American dollar and its slide versus other currencies. However, in the U.S. versus Canada exchange rate, USD vs CAD, the Canadian dollar has been losing ground while the American dollar has been rising in value. This is likely due to the poor economic environment in Canada. While I believe international diversification is smart, I don’t believe Canada offers good diversification at this time. Canada’s fate remains tied to the United States, thus reducing diversification potential.

BBCA Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

One of the things I noticed right off the bat is the low adjusted expense ratio, which is a mere 0.19%. I’ve looked at a variety of foreign market ETFs and rates of 0.5% or greater are common. This is one of the cheaper ETFs I’ve seen. That said, BBCA is more expensive than SPY’s 0.095%. Where BBCA really excels compared to SPY is in the PE department. Morningstar data notes that BBCA has a portfolio-wide PE ratio of 17.83, compared to SPY’s 22.36. It should be noted that key European market ETFs, like Italy, often have PE ratios below 15. BBCA also wins in the PS ratio at 2.52 to SPY’s 3.20.

Unfortunately, the weak economic environment and tough negotiations with the Trump Administration increase risks substantially. This alone should result in lower PE ratios, and in this situation, I’d argue that the PE and PS ratios for BBCA are too high given the headwinds. SPY may not seem like a better deal, but I think it has comparable upside with substantially lower risks.

BBCA has a hefty amount of portfolio weight in financials. This is pretty common for foreign ETFs, and by and large, this lowers my preferences for many of them. I’d prefer to see higher diversification. SPY tracks the S&P 500, and owing to the vast valuations of leading tech companies, it’s heavily concentrated in tech. As such, from a diversification perspective, I believe investors need to look beyond SPY, adding other ETFs not as heavily concentrated in technology. In both cases, the top holding in both portfolios weighs in at about 7.5%, and the top 10 stocks make up ~36% for SPY and 42% for BBCA, suggesting a solid degree overall of diversification

As for dividends, BBCA offers a decent 1.77% TTM yield and a 5-year CAGR north of 5%. SPY offers just a ~1% dividend yield along with a ~5% 5-year CAGR. While I wouldn’t rely on either as a major income investment, BBCA’s ~2% yield is reasonably attractive, especially given the ETF’s low costs.

The 5-year tracking error for BBCA is a mere 1.85%, which is pretty good in my book, especially for foreign market ETFs. Many such ETFs have double-digit tracking errors. Of course, SPY is a gold standard as far as tracking errors are concerned, with a 5-year error rate of just 0.07%. As for ratings, BBCA gets high marks, as shown below. I agree with these grades in and of themselves. BBCA on paper is attractive. However, the larger macroeconomic issues, which can be difficult to catch with data, keep me at a sell rating. SPY also gets mostly good marks, although risk gets a B-, which I personally feel is too low a grade.

BBCA

SPY

Why I Could Be Wrong

President Trump changes his mind rather frequently. While it appears that both sides are digging in, Trump could simply change his mind, especially with inflation heating up and the war in Iran likely to add to American financial deficits and inflation. With midterms fast approaching, Trump may decide to pause or end tariffs, likely hoping that doing so will cool inflation. Further, given that 51% of Americans oppose tariffs on Canada, Trump may decide to shore up voters by dropping the tariffs.

SPY remains a buy for right now, meaning I don’t believe investors need to exit the market. However, I do feel like the economy has formed a sort of house of cards, and a major event could quickly spark a sustained and deep sell-off. If so, hopefully I catch wind before everything is set in motion.

Key Takeaway: Now Isn’t The Time To Go Canadian

I hope that Canada comes out of negotiations with the Trump Administration with a good deal. That said, we can’t be sure that that will happen and there’s a high chance that President Trump will continue to stir the pot. Still, the approaching midterms provide at least some hope that President Trump will ease up. On the whole, however, Canada and thus BBCA face too strong of headwinds for me to invest right now. SPY remains a buy, but investors should closely monitor the economy for signs of trouble.

data.title: Diana Shipping: Proposed Genco Acquisition Unlikely To Benefit Shares - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Diana Shipping Inc. or "Diana Shipping" (DSX, DSX.PR.B, DSX.WS) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Please note also that Diana Shipping, Genco Shipping & Trading (GNK) and Star Bulk Carriers (SBLK) are part of our coverage universe at Value Investor's Edge.

Reflecting On A Bad Call

Eighteen months ago, I upgraded shares of leading dry bulk shipper Diana Shipping from "Hold" to "Buy" due to a number of factors:

  • improvements in the dry bulk shipping market.
  • deeply discounted valuation.
  • expectations for the company's generous quarterly dividend to remain safe for the time being.

Unfortunately, this turned out to be disastrous call, as the company reduced the quarterly dividend by 87% just two months after my article was published. While this was partially offset by a generous self-tender offer, mediocre market conditions and the Trump Tariffs shock resulted in the stock price dropping by more than 40% until April 2025.

While shares have recovered all losses in recent quarters, Diana Shipping has underperformed peers quite meaningfully:

Disappointing Fourth Quarter Results

Last month, the company reported another set of disappointing quarterly results with both revenues and profitability falling short of expectations.

While Diana Shipping's conservative chartering approach results in rather stable average daily time charter equivalent ("TCE") rates, increased operating expenses and the sale of some older vessels have negatively impacted core earnings and cash flows in recent quarters.

In fact, fourth quarter operating cash flow of $7.3 million represented a new multi-year low for the company. Diana Shipping ended the quarter with $122.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and liquid investments as well as $636.1 million in debt.

Proposed Acquisition Of Genco Shipping & Trading

In recent quarters, the company has built a sizeable position in shares of larger competitor Genco Shipping & Trading or "Genco Shipping." According to regulatory filings, Diana Shipping has spent $103.5 million to accumulate a 14.8% stake in Genco Shipping.

Four months ago, the company announced a proposal to acquire the remaining shares for $20.60 in cash which was rejected by Genco Shipping's board of directors.

In response, Diana Shipping launched a proxy fight by nominating six director candidates for election to Genco Shipping's board of directors.

Two weeks ago, the company increased its offer to $23.50 per share with $1.43 billion in committed financing arranged by DNB Carnegie and Nordea with participation from leading international banks, including DNB, Nordea, BNP Paribas, Standard Chartered, Deutsche Bank and Danske Bank.

In addition, market leader Star Bulk Carriers has entered into a definitive agreement with Diana Shipping to acquire 16 Genco vessels for $470.5 million in cash upon completion of the proposed transaction.

Together, the fully committed financing and the definitive agreement with Star Bulk provide a clear and executable path to complete the acquisition of Genco’s outstanding shares, refinance existing indebtedness, and pay related transaction expenses.

The committed financing is fully underwritten and not conditioned on completion of the Star Bulk transaction.

On Thursday, Genco Shipping's board of directors rejected the revised proposal due to, among other things,

  • the lack of an appropriate premium to net asset value ("NAV").
  • a $0.32 billion disparity between the amount of committed financing stated in Diana Shipping's press release and the actual commitment letter filed with the SEC.
  • the proposed "fire sale" of 16 vessels to Star Bulk Carriers for an aggregate price of $470.5 million.

While Genco Shipping's objections carry some merit, seeking a premium to NAV may be perceived as overly ambitious given that its primary peers trade at significant discounts:

In addition, Diana Shipping's agreement to sell 16 vessels at an estimated 10-15% discount to Star Bulk Carriers should not be a cause for concern for Genco Shipping's board of directors as long as they manage to negotiate the best possible price for the company's equity holders.

Once a transaction has been agreed on and approved by shareholders, it doesn't really matter anymore what exactly Diana Shipping does with the acquired vessels, at least not for Genco Shipping's shareholders.

On Friday, Diana Shipping commented on the rejection of the revised proposal by Genco Shipping's board of directors. In addition to the issues discussed by me above, the company clarified that $331 million in additional committed financing are related to a partial refinancing of Diana Shipping's existing debt.

Genco’s actions lead us to conclude that this Board and management team are more focused on entrenching themselves than maximizing value for their shareholders.

We, therefore, have no choice but to proceed with our effort to elect to the Genco Board independent directors who will act in the best interest of all shareholders by exploring all meaningful opportunities for value creation.

In my opinion, Genco Shipping's board of directors should engage with Diana Shipping and negotiate a transaction price closer to the company's estimated net asset value of approximately $25 per share.

The combined company would become the second largest U.S. exchange-traded dry bulk shipping pure play with an on-the-water fleet of 66 vessels.

In addition, Genco Shipping's lower leverage would result in a stronger balance sheet for the enlarged Diana Shipping.

However, without changes to the company's overly conservative chartering strategy and a return to more generous dividend payments, I do not expect Diana Shipping's NAV discount to decrease meaningfully.

For my part, I would rather consider an investment in industry leader Star Bulk Carriers which is also part of our Income Model Portfolio at Value Investor's Edge.

Assuming Diana Shipping manages to acquire Genco Shipping and second hand vessel values remaining stable, Star Bulk Carriers would increase its fleet size by more than 10% at a material discount to estimated net asset value.

Please note also that Star Bulk Carriers expects to distribute 100% of excess cash flow to common shareholders on a quarterly basis. Given solid market conditions, an annual dividend yield of 10% might be in the cards for this year.

As a potential acquisition of Genco Shipping & Trading is not likely to result in substantially increased shareholder capital returns or changes to Diana Shipping's chartering approach, I am downgrading the company's common shares from Buy to Hold.

Bottom Line

Diana Shipping's financial results remain impacted by a combination of decreased fleet size and higher operating expenses as well as the company's overly conservative chartering strategy.

In recent quarters, Diana Shipping has built a substantial stake in larger peer Genco Shipping & Trading and offered to acquire the remainder of the company at a price of $23.50 per share in cash.

As Genco Shipping's board has been reluctant to engage with Diana Shipping, a proxy fight appears inevitable.

However, even if Diana Shipping manages to acquire its larger competitor, the transaction is not likely to result in substantially increased shareholder capital returns or changes to Diana Shipping's overly conservative chartering approach.

Consequently, I am downgrading the company's common shares from Buy to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
